title: Cobalt blue underglaze painting on stoneware

glossaryTerms.definition: clay that has been through the first firing (cone 06, approximately 1000 degrees C) and is now hard but unglazed and porous
a pyrometric measure of heat-work: cone 06 is approximately 1000 degrees C, cone 6 is approximately 1220 degrees C
the drying stage where clay holds its shape but is still damp enough to trim, carve, and join

content.text: Silica dust is the chronic studio hazard. Silicosis is irreversible. Mix wet whenever possible. Wear a P100 respirator any time raw glaze powders are open. Wet-clean only: no sweeping, no compressed air, no dry sanding indoors.
Ventilation matters. Either a downdraft vent on the kiln or an open window with an extractor pulling air past the kiln. Never open a hot kiln below approximately 80 degrees C.
Note: the pre-mixed underglaze used here contains cobalt compounds already suspended in a safe medium. Raw cobalt carbonate requires gloves and a respirator; never use it near food preparation.
Brushing cobalt blue underglaze onto
bisque

ware and covering it with a transparent glaze is the classic blue-and-white stoneware technique. The underglaze colour is sealed beneath the transparent glaze layer during the glaze firing, becoming permanent and food-safe. The approach uses a pre-mixed cobalt blue underglaze, which contains the colorant already suspended in a stable clay medium, so no handling of raw cobalt powder is involved. Two or three brush coats build opacity; lighter washes produce softer, watercolour-like areas. The piece fires to
cone
6 (approximately 1220 degrees C).
When to use it

Apply underglaze to bisqueware for the most control. Bisque is stable and porous without being fragile. Underglaze can also go onto leather-hard or bone-dry greenware. Bisque is easier because a damp sponge wipes away unwanted brushwork before the transparent overglaze goes on. The technique works on any smooth stoneware form large enough to carry a design. Heavily textured surfaces hold underglaze in the recesses and give a different result from flat surfaces.
leather-hard

or bone-dry greenware, but bisque allows brushwork corrections: a damp sponge wipes away unwanted strokes before the transparent overglaze is applied. The technique works on any smooth stoneware form with a surface area large enough to carry a design. Heavily textured surfaces hold underglaze in the recesses and show a different result from flat surfaces.
What you need

materials.name: Cobalt blue underglaze, pre-mixed
materials.quantity: small jar, enough for two or three coats on one piece

content.text: Step by step

Wipe the bisqueware with a clean damp sponge to remove any dust. Allow to dry for ten minutes. Dust prevents underglaze adhesion and causes the colour to lift when the transparent glaze is applied over it.

Stir the underglaze thoroughly. Decant a small amount into a clean palette or shallow dish. Underglaze settles in the jar and a stirred-but-unpoured jar will still deliver variable consistency brush to brush as you work.

Apply the first coat with the appropriate brush for each area: the liner brush for fine lines, the mop brush for medium areas, the hake brush for wide washes. Load the brush generously but avoid pooling underglaze in recesses or at the base of walls, where it will run during firing. Work with a single stroke per area where possible: bisque is porous and pulls moisture from the brush quickly, so multiple strokes over the same wet area lift the previous coat.

Allow the first coat to dry completely before applying the second. Cobalt blue underglaze appears pale grey-blue in its unfired state: two coats of a mid-tone blue produces a finished fired blue of reasonable depth. Three coats produces a rich, deep blue under a clear glaze.

Leave areas of bare bisque where you want the natural stoneware colour to show through the transparent glaze. These bare areas will appear as cream to tan in the finished piece. The contrast between painted areas and bare clay is one of the most effective elements in blue-and-white work.

Apply the transparent glaze over the dried underglaze by dipping or brushing. Brushed glaze over underglaze requires three coats applied with a wide hake brush in alternating directions; dipping is more consistent. Ensure the glaze coat covers the underglaze evenly without gaps, which would leave the underglaze unprotected and result in a rough, non-food-safe surface.

Clean the foot ring of any glaze drips. Fire to cone 6 (approximately 1220 degrees C). The underglaze colour deepens and fixes during the firing: the pale grey-blue of the unfired piece becomes a saturated cobalt blue under the transparent glaze.
What it looks like when it works

A successful cobalt blue underglaze result shows a rich blue design with clean edges, sitting under a transparent glaze that appears slightly glassy rather than matte. The brush strokes are visible in the fired surface, giving the piece a hand-painted quality. The blue is deep where three coats were applied, lighter and more translucent where one coat or a thin wash was used. Bare clay areas show as cream through the transparent glaze, providing a warm contrast to the blue.
Common mistakes
Only one coat of underglaze: a single coat produces a washed-out, patchy blue after firing. Two coats is the minimum for a solid mid-tone; three for a deep rich result.

Underglaze applied over dusty bisque: the underglaze lifts when touched or when the transparent glaze goes on. The dust layer stops it bonding to the clay body. Always wipe the bisque clean before applying underglaze.

Applying transparent glaze too thickly over underglaze: a thick glaze coat runs during firing and can carry the underglaze with it, bleeding the edges of painted lines. Apply glaze at a consistent thickness using the standard dip time.

Leaving gaps in the transparent glaze coverage: any underglaze left unprotected by the transparent glaze layer remains a rough, porous surface after firing and is not food-safe. Check coverage carefully before loading the kiln.
